name: BYOVD - Vulnerable Driver File Drop to Non-Standard Location
description: Detects .sys driver files written to non-standard locations outside C:\Windows\System32\drivers, which is a common indicator of BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) attacks used by Akira ransomware and similar threats.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
query: file where event.action == "creation" and file.extension == "sys" and not file.path : ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\drivers\\*", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\DriverStore\\*", "C:\\Windows\\SysWOW64\\drivers\\*", "C:\\Windows\\WinSxS\\*", "C:\\Windows\\SoftwareDistribution\\*") and not process.executable : ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\drvinst.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\msiexec.exe", "C:\\Program Files\\*", "C:\\Program Files (x86)\\*")
note:
## Triage
- Verify the process dropping the .sys file is legitimate
- Check the driver file hash against Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist
- Look for subsequent sc.exe service creation commands
- Check if the driver is signed and by whom

## False Positives
- Legitimate driver installations by hardware vendors
- Windows Update or DISM operations
- Third-party AV or security tool driver deployment
false_positives: Legitimate driver installations by hardware vendors

name: BYOVD - Known Vulnerable Driver File Name Detected
description: Detects creation of files matching known vulnerable driver names commonly abused in BYOVD attacks including rwdrv.sys (ThrottleStop), mhyprot2.sys, dbutil_2_3.sys, and others.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder
note:
## Triage
- Immediately investigate the source process
- Check if a corresponding kernel service was created
- Determine if this is part of a legitimate software installation
- Correlate with other BYOVD indicators (service creation, registry tampering)

## False Positives
- ThrottleStop legitimate use (rwdrv.sys) - rare in enterprise environments
- Genshin Impact anti-cheat (mhyprot2.sys) on gaming endpoints
false_positives: ThrottleStop legitimate use on personal systems
Genshin Impact anti-cheat on gaming endpoints

name: BYOVD - Kernel-Mode Service Creation via sc.exe
description: Detects creation of kernel-mode services via sc.exe pointing to driver binaries outside standard driver directories. Akira ransomware creates services like mgdsrv and KMHLPSVC to load vulnerable and malicious drivers.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

query: process where event.action == "start" and process.name : "sc.exe" and process.args : "create" and process.args : "kernel" and not process.args : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\drivers\\*"
note:
## Triage
- Identify the service name and binary path from sc.exe arguments
- Verify the driver binary is signed and not on the vulnerable driver blocklist
- Check for associated driver file creation events
- Determine if the parent process is legitimate

## False Positives
- Legitimate driver installations during software setup
- Hardware vendor driver installers
false_positives: Legitimate driver installations during software setup

name: Windows Defender Registry Tampering - Defense Evasion
description: Detects modifications to Windows Defender registry keys used to disable anti-spyware, real-time monitoring, or tamper protection. This is a key step in the Akira ransomware BYOVD attack chain.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

query: registry where event.action == "modification" and registry.path : ("HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\DisableAntiSpyware", "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableRealtimeMonitoring", "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Features\\TamperProtection", "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\DisableAntiVirus", "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableBehaviorMonitoring", "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableOnAccessProtection", "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableScanOnRealtimeEnable") and not process.executable : ("C:\\Windows\\System32\\svchost.exe", "C:\\ProgramData\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Platform\\*\\MsMpEng.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\gpupdate.exe", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\wuauclt.exe")
note:
## Triage
- This is a CRITICAL detection - Windows Defender is being deliberately disabled
- Identify the process modifying the registry immediately
- Check for associated BYOVD indicators (driver drops, kernel service creation)
- Verify with change management whether GPO changes were planned
- Initiate incident response if unauthorized

## False Positives
- Group Policy updates applying Defender configuration
- Intune/SCCM managed Defender policy changes
- IT troubleshooting with documented change tickets
false_positives: Group Policy updates applying Defender configuration
IT troubleshooting with documented change tickets

name: PowerShell Execution Policy Bypass Targeting Defender Settings
description: Detects PowerShell launched with -ExecutionPolicy Bypass that targets Windows Defender registry keys or configuration. This pattern is used by Akira ransomware to disable security controls after gaining elevated privileges via BYOVD.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

query: process where event.action == "start" and process.name : ("powershell.exe", "pwsh.exe") and process.args : "-ExecutionPolicy" and process.args : "Bypass" and process.args : ("*DisableAntiSpyware*", "*Windows Defender*", "*DisableRealtimeMonitoring*", "*TamperProtection*", "*Set-MpPreference*", "*defender_disable*", "*WindowStyle*Hidden*")
note:
## Triage
- Review the full command line and script contents
- Check Script Block Logging (Event ID 4104) for detailed script content
- Determine if this was an authorized IT action
- Correlate with driver drops and service creation events

## False Positives
- IT automation scripts that configure Defender settings with change management approval
false_positives: IT automation scripts that configure Defender settings with change management approval

name: Suspicious Kernel-Mode Service Installed (Event 7045)
description: Detects installation of kernel-mode services from non-standard paths via Windows Event ID 7045. This captures the service installation step of BYOVD attacks regardless of the method used to create the service.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Create or Modify System Process: Windows Service

query: any where event.code == "7045" and winlog.event_data.ServiceType : "kernel mode driver" and not winlog.event_data.ImagePath : ("\\SystemRoot\\System32\\drivers\\*", "C:\\Windows\\System32\\drivers\\*", "system32\\DRIVERS\\*")
note:
## Triage
- Identify the service name and image path
- Verify the driver binary integrity and signature
- Check if the driver hash is on the Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist
- Look for preceding driver file creation events

## False Positives
- Third-party security products installing kernel drivers
- Legitimate hardware driver installations
false_positives: Third-party security products installing kernel drivers

name: BYOVD Multi-Stage Attack Sequence - Driver Drop Followed by Service Creation
description: Detects the sequential pattern of a driver file being dropped to a non-standard location followed by kernel service creation, which is characteristic of BYOVD attacks like the Akira ransomware attack chain.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

query:
sequence by host.id with maxspan=30m
  [file where event.action == "creation" and file.extension == "sys" and not file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\drivers\\*"]
  [process where event.action == "start" and process.name : "sc.exe" and process.args : "create" and process.args : "kernel"]
note:
## Triage
- This is a high-confidence multi-stage detection
- The sequence of driver file creation followed by kernel service creation strongly indicates a BYOVD attack
- Immediately investigate the source host and user account
- Check for Windows Defender registry modifications as a follow-up action
- Initiate incident response if unauthorized

## False Positives
- Very rare - this sequence is uncommon outside of BYOVD attacks
- Possible during legitimate driver installation workflows with custom installers
false_positives: Legitimate driver installation workflows with custom installers

name: BYOVD Full Attack Chain - Driver Drop, Service Creation, and Defender Tampering
description: Detects the complete BYOVD attack chain: driver file dropped, kernel service created, followed by Windows Defender registry tampering. This three-stage sequence is a hallmark of the Akira ransomware attack chain.
author: F0RT1KA Defense Guidance Builder

technique.name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

query:
sequence by host.id with maxspan=1h
  [file where event.action == "creation" and file.extension == "sys" and not file.path : "C:\\Windows\\System32\\drivers\\*"]
  [process where event.action == "start" and process.name : "sc.exe" and process.args : "create" and process.args : "kernel"]
  [registry where event.action == "modification" and registry.path : ("HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\DisableAntiSpyware", "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Policies\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Real-Time Protection\\DisableRealtimeMonitoring", "HKLM\\SOFTWARE\\Microsoft\\Windows Defender\\Features\\TamperProtection")]
note:
## Triage
- CRITICAL: This detection represents the complete BYOVD attack kill chain
- All three stages have been observed: driver deployment, service creation, Defender tampering
- Immediately isolate the affected host
- Begin full incident response procedures
- Check for ransomware deployment indicators

## False Positives
- Extremely unlikely to produce false positives due to three-stage correlation
false_positives: Extremely unlikely - requires three-stage correlated activity
